focal_character_timelines.selection_reason: Aligned with the movie's ICRP character set.
focal_character_timelines.task3_relevance: Directly aligned with the same key character used in ICRP.

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy Kaufman breaks character, reveals the biopic is actually good and covers his life from childhood to death, then starts a projector to begin the real story, retreating into nostalgia.
timeline_nodes.role_in_context: narrator and guide of his own life story
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy stops a fake biopic, reveals the real one includes his entire life including his death, and starts telling the story on his own terms.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: to share his life story only with an audience that will understand it
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Andy begins narrating his life story, starting from childhood, after dismissing a false version of the film
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: the mention of his death raises questions about when and how it will happen
evidence_quotes: I was only kidding about the movie... it's actually PRETTY GOOD! It shows everything... from me as a little boy until my death --
(his eyes pop; he covers his mouth) Oops!! I wasn't supposed to talk about that!
Andy turns to a primitive 16mm PROJECTOR and turns it on. WHIR!
Okay! Just my friends are left. I wanted to get rid of those other people... they would have laughed in the wrong places.

status_updates: assumes role of narrator
abandons Foreign Man persona

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Stanley demands Andy stop performing for imaginary audiences, insisting real people are needed; Andy reluctantly agrees after Stanley emphasizes live audiences over plaster walls.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy is confronted by his father for performing to an imaginary audience and eventually concedes that a real audience is necessary for performance.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: to perform, even without a real audience
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: acknowledges that performance requires real people, not imagined ones, after his father insists on the necessity of a live audience
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: tension between internal imagination and external validation
evidence_quotes: STANLEY Andy, this has to stop. Our house isn't a television station. There is not a camera in that wall.
STANLEY No! That is NOT an audience! That is PLASTER! An audience is people made of flesh! They -- live and breathe! Got it?!
Andy thinks, considering his options. Then, he nods.

relation_updates: strained dynamic with Stanley established
status_updates: shifts from solitary play to needing live audience

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy Kaufman, 26, performs his quirky song act to a disinterested, drunk audience in a small New York club; after faint applause, he packs up as the manager expresses irritation.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy performs his children's song act to a disengaged, intoxicated audience who respond with minimal, perfunctory participation and faint applause, highlighting a mismatch between his performance style and the nightclub setting.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: perform authentically despite audience indifference
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: continues to complete his act and pack up his props after a poorly received performance, maintaining composure amid lack of engagement
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: lack of audience connection threatens career viability in competitive performance venues
evidence_quotes: ANDY "And the bird says..."
DRUNK AUDIENCE TWEET!!
ANDY "And the lion goes..."
DRUNK AUDIENCE ROAR!!

relation_updates: manager views Andy as ineffective performer
status_updates: act fails to generate audience energy
professional standing weakened

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: George is intrigued by the performer, whispers to Budd who thinks he's Lithuanian and incomprehensible, but George admires his impressive Elvis act.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy performs as Foreign Man, struggling to connect with the audience, then transforms into a precise and confident Elvis impersonation that impresses the crowd.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: establish stage presence through contrast between awkwardness and charisma
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: emerges as a performer capable of dramatic transformation and audience engagement
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: audience and industry reception remains uncertain
evidence_quotes: when Andy suddenly, awkwardly steps on stage. He is in character as Foreign Man.
Andy tentatively grabs the mike. The stagefright is agony. ANDY (AS FOREIGN MAN) Now? Now...? (looking around) Tank you veddy much.
And now... I would like to imitate the Elvis Presley.
With a flourish, Andy pulls tape off his pants -- revealing rhinestones.

status_updates: performs publicly for the first time in character
demonstrates transformative performance ability

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy impresses George with his unconventional act and dedication, convincing him despite initial doubts; George agrees to represent Andy, seeing potential brilliance in his insanity.
timeline_nodes.role_in_context: Andy convinces George to represent him by demonstrating controlled eccentricity during their meeting
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy communicates his ambitions and unconventional approach through deliberate, odd behaviors, leading George to agree to represent him.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: become the biggest star in the world
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: officially enters into a professional representation agreement with George
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: uncertainty about where Andy's act fits in the entertainment industry
evidence_quotes: You see, I want to be the biggest star in the world.
I'm not a comedian. I have no talent. (he shrugs) I'm a song-and-dance man.
George looks up at Andy -- and inexplicably there is a giant MOIST BOOGER hanging from Andy's nostril. George cringes.
After I'm famous, I can sell these as \"Worn by Andy Kaufman.\"

relation_updates: forms professional partnership with George
status_updates: officially represented by talent agent

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy places a boom box playing the Mighty Mouse theme, stands motionless, and confuses the audience while the Kaufmans grow alarmed.
timeline_nodes.role_in_context: subversive performance art on live television
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy places a boom box playing the Mighty Mouse theme on stage and stands completely still, deliberately performing nothing as the music plays, which confuses the audience and alarms his family.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: challenge conventional expectations of entertainment
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: adopts a deliberate, non-performative stance that rejects traditional comedic delivery in favor of conceptual expression
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: tension between audience expectations and artistic authenticity
evidence_quotes: Andy puts the boom box down and blares it loudly.
The THEME FROM "MIGHTY MOUSE" plays -- but Andy just blankly stands there.
He's purposefully doing nothing.
The audience is puzzled. The Kaufmans are alarmed.

relation_updates: family becomes alarmed by his behavior
audience confused, creating distance
status_updates: shifts from passive freeze to active artistic statement

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy reluctantly agrees to star in the sitcom after George's outburst, proposing impossible terms including guest spots for Tony Clifton, a rival entertainer he owes a debt to.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy agrees to star in a sitcom after initially refusing, then imposes a set of difficult terms including guest appearances for Tony Clifton.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: I want to create my own material
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Andy agrees to the sitcom role but attempts to maintain control by setting demanding conditions.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: Whether ABC will accept Andy's demands, particularly the inclusion of Tony Clifton.
evidence_quotes: No to the whole thing. None of it sounds good.
I'm not interested. I want to create my own material.
Okay. Fine, I'll do it. (beat) But I have a few terms.
If I'm the new Fonz... ABC's just gonna have to give me what I want. (a sarcastic FONZIE IMPRESSION) Heyyyyyy!

relation_updates: Andy asserts power over George
status_updates: Andy agrees to star in a network sitcom under self-defined conditions

timeline_nodes.scene_title: 28、INT. MAMA ROMA'S, KITCHEN - LATER THAT NIGHT
timeline_nodes.scene_summary: George confronts Andy, who reveals the prank with Bob Zmuda, exposing their deception; George realizes the truth and begins to laugh as the hoax unfolds.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy reveals that his identity as Tony Gorsky was a fabricated performance, exposing the prank to George with Bob Zmuda's appearance.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: defending the artistic value of the hoax
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Andy's orchestration of the Tony persona as a deliberate act of performance is confirmed, and George begins to accept the prank.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: ethical implications of the prank remain contested.
evidence_quotes: He pauses... slowly turns around... and is Andy.
ANDY (in jolly spirits) It's good old-fashioned entertainment. Everyone loves a villain.
ZMUDA Hey Andy, good show.
GEORGE So your name's not Gorsky. ZMUDA Don't believe everything you hear.

relation_updates: establishes collaborative relationship with Bob Zmuda
shifts dynamic with George from adversarial to revealed trickster-target
status_updates: confirms Andy's control over the Tony persona as part of an act

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Mimi admires the sunset, but Andy dismisses it and abruptly proposes marriage, unsettling her further and prompting her to say she wants to go home.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy dismisses Mimi's appreciation of the sunset and abruptly proposes they drive to Tijuana to get married, unsettling her.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: create instant intimacy through unconventional means
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Mimi expresses fear and a desire to go home, withdrawing from the interaction.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: his need for connection vs. self-sabotaging behavior
evidence_quotes: ANDY (he shrugs dully) I've never understood that. It's just... getting dark.
But I like you! Hey! Why don't we fill the car with gas, drive to Tijuana, and GET MARRIED???
MIMI I think I wanna go home.

relation_updates: relationship with Mimi deteriorates due to inappropriate proposal
status_updates: emotional misalignment with peer becomes evident

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy finishes reading to a nearly empty, silent audience, with only a few asleep; he exits unnoticed, met by Zmuda snoring in the wings.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy completes his reading to an unresponsive, mostly sleeping audience and exits without acknowledgment.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: complete the artistic performance regardless of reception
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: the performance ends with no audience response, leaving Andy isolated and unseen
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: whether art has value without an audience
evidence_quotes: Andy somberly shuts the book. ANDY (BRITISH) (cont'd) The End.
A moment of quiet personal euphoria. Andy looks enraptured, the man who has just climbed Everest.
Andy looks out... and realizes the few audience members are asleep.
Andy shrugs, then shuffles off-stage.

relation_updates: audience becomes irrelevant through disengagement
status_updates: performance concludes
public recognition fails

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy is repeatedly washing his hands with liquid soap in a compulsive, repetitive manner.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy engages in repetitive, manic handwashing, indicating obsessive or anxiety-driven behavior.
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Andy is visibly distressed, absorbed in a cycle of repeated handwashing with no clear resolution.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: source of Andy's anxiety remains unaddressed
evidence_quotes: Andy is manically washing his hands.
Using liquid soap from the dispenser, then rubbing his hands under the water.
Then more liquid soap.
More rubbing.

status_updates: exhibits compulsive behavior

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy confirms he has rare lung cancer, distressing Lynne, who cries and asks about treatment and family; George remains skeptical, demanding truth, which Andy affirms.
timeline_nodes.role_in_context: major personal revelation and relational test
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy announces he has lung cancer, prompting emotional and skeptical reactions from friends.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: seek support and validation
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Andy has publicly declared a cancer diagnosis, leading to emotional concern from Lynne and doubt from George.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: George questions the truth of the diagnosis and demands confirmation.
evidence_quotes: ANDY I have cancer.
ANDY No, it's true. I have lung cancer.
GEORGE Andy... you look me in the eye, and tell me this is true.
ANDY George -- it's true.

relation_updates: Lynne responds with emotional care and concern
George demands truth, testing their bond
Zmuda's exploitative reaction creates tension
status_updates: public persona now intersects with private suffering

focal_character_timelines.timeline_summary: Andy Kaufman's journey traces a relentless pursuit of authenticity through performance, defined by a refusal to conform to audience or industry expectations. He evolves from a solitary child performer into a provocateur who blurs reality and fiction, using personas like Foreign Man and Tony Clifton to challenge norms and maintain artistic control. His need to shock and connect drives both triumphs and breakdowns, culminating in a final confrontation with mortality that remains framed as performance. Throughout, his relationships with George, Lynne, and Zmuda reflect the tension between his constructed identities and the desire for genuine human connection.

focal_character_timelines.selection_reason: Aligned with the movie's ICRP character set.
focal_character_timelines.task3_relevance: Directly aligned with the same key character used in ICRP.

timeline_nodes.scene_title: 28、INT. MAMA ROMA'S, KITCHEN - LATER THAT NIGHT
timeline_nodes.scene_summary: George confronts Andy, who reveals the prank with Bob Zmuda, exposing their deception; George realizes the truth and begins to laugh as the hoax unfolds.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Bob Zmuda is publicly revealed as Andy Kaufman's collaborator in the Tony Clifton hoax, confirming his involvement in the deception.
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Zmuda's role as Andy's accomplice in the prank is acknowledged by Andy and witnessed by George, making it clear he is a key participant in the performance.
evidence_quotes: then Bob strolls over. His real name is BOB ZMUDA.
ZMUDA Hey Andy, good show.
ANDY Oh George, this is Bob Zmuda. Bob and I have been buddies for years.
He and Andy grin naughtily at each other.

relation_updates: establishes partnership with Andy
contrasts with George's shock
status_updates: publicly acknowledged as Andy's longtime buddy
positioned as a key creative accomplice

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: After a show, Andy tells Zmuda he wants to quit, criticizing its quality, but Zmuda argues the audience's trust gives him power to manipulate them later.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda convinces Andy to stay on the show by arguing that the audience's trust can be used to manipulate them in the future.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: maintain Andy's public persona to exploit audience credibility
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Andy remains on the show, persuaded by Zmuda's argument about leveraging audience trust for future deception.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: whether the audience's trust will be broken as planned
evidence_quotes: ANDY I'm gonna quit.
ANDY Each show is worse than the next.
ZMUDA Are you nuts?! 40 million people watch you every week!

relation_updates: asserts influence over Andy
status_updates: emerges as the architect of long-term pranks

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy demands and forcibly takes a small book from Zmuda, who resists and cringes as Andy insists it's necessary.
timeline_nodes.role_in_context: reluctant participant in a performance escalation
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Andy forcibly takes a small book from Zmuda despite verbal resistance, signaling a shift toward physical coercion in their interaction.
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Zmuda is no longer a willing participant; he loses control of the book and reacts physically with discomfort.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: what the book contains and why Andy insists it is necessary
evidence_quotes: ZMUDA (startled) No! Andy, don't do it --
Andy fiercely GRABS a small book from Zmuda.
Zmuda cringes.

relation_updates: shows growing tension in their partnership
reveals Zmuda's discomfort with Andy's methods
status_updates: loses possession of the book
physically reacts with cringe

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Zmuda convinces a hesitant Andy to enter the Mustang Ranch brothel, reassuring him despite Andy's concerns about morality and his mother's opinion.
timeline_nodes.role_in_context: guide and reassurer in a morally ambiguous situation
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda persuades Andy to enter the brothel by addressing his concerns about morality and cleanliness with dismissive reassurance.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: get Andy to go through with the brothel visit
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Andy agrees to enter the Mustang Ranch after Zmuda's reassurances, remaining visibly hesitant but compliant.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: Andy's discomfort with the moral and personal implications of visiting a brothel
evidence_quotes: I dunno about this... (worried) What will my mother think?
She'll say, "Now my son is a man."
It's so dirty.
Nah. The girls sponge off between johns.

relation_updates: reinforces role as Andy's trusted companion
status_updates: actively enables Andy's experience at the brothel

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Zmuda learns Andy, his supposedly inexperienced friend, is a regular client who uses aliases and disguises, leaving Zmuda shocked and realizing he's been deceived.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda learns that Andy, whom he believed was inexperienced with sex workers, is actually a frequent client who uses aliases and disguises, contradicting Andy's nervous performance and revealing a deliberate deception.
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Zmuda is stunned and realizes he has been misled about Andy's true behavior, shifting his perception of Andy's authenticity
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: reconciling Andy's public vulnerability with private duplicity
evidence_quotes: ZMUDA This is a big day. It's my friend's first time with a prostitute.
MADAM (mocking) What're you talking about? Andy comes here almost every weekend.
MADAM Oh, he doesn't always call himself that. Sometimes he's Tony, and wears a tux.
He reaches the door -- then gives Zmuda a nervous look.

relation_updates: trust in Andy is undermined
status_updates: realizes he has been misled about Andy's true behavior

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: George challenges Andy and Zmuda on the point of their elaborate joke; Andy explains he must constantly shock audiences, blurring reality, leaving George unsettled by the deception.
timeline_nodes.role_in_context: performer and defender of the Tony Clifton persona
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda is revealed as the person under the Tony Clifton makeup and expresses enthusiasm for continuing the role, affirming his active participation in the performance hoax.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: continue the Tony Clifton performance as a shared private joke
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: confirmed as an active participant in Andy Kaufman's performance art, not merely an assistant
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: ethical ambiguity of deceiving audiences and friends
evidence_quotes: and inside, taking off the rubber Tony makeup, is Zmuda.
ZMUDA Sure! But WE think it's kickass! Now I get to be Tony. I get to dump the glass of water on someone else's head!
GEORGE So you've got this big elaborate joke, which is really only funny to two people in the universe. (dry) You... and you.

relation_updates: asserts alignment with Andy against George's skepticism
status_updates: physically revealed as Tony Clifton

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Lawler performs a piledriver on Andy, causing disqualification; Andy lies unconscious, stretchered out amid chaos while Lawler celebrates and the crowd erupts.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda shifts from observer to active protector by urgently calling for a stretcher when Andy is injured.

status_updates: takes charge in chaotic situation

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy is carried into the dressing room by Coliseum employees as Zmuda clears out reporters and locks the door behind them.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda clears reporters and onlookers from the dressing room and locks the door to secure the space.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: isolate Andy from public and media scrutiny
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: establishes control over access to Andy in the immediate aftermath of the incident
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: whether the injury is real or part of the act remains unclear

He slams the door
locks it

status_updates: assumes control of the immediate environment

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy confirms he has rare lung cancer, distressing Lynne, who cries and asks about treatment and family; George remains skeptical, demanding truth, which Andy affirms.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda responds to Andy's cancer announcement by immediately suggesting it be turned into a performance, with exaggerated scenarios and comedic staging.

timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: whether Zmuda believes Andy's diagnosis or suspects it as another hoax

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Zmuda discusses Andy Kaufman's terminal cancer and frequent crystal sessions with an administrator concerned about the resort's reputation, leading to Zmuda being asked to leave.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda is asked to leave the spa after defending Andy's right to continue crystal treatments, despite his personal disbelief in their efficacy.

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy, initially shocked and disillusioned upon discovering Jun Roxas's fraud, erupts into uncontrollable laughter, finding profound, liberating joy in the absurdity of the deception.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: Zmuda confronts the futility of Andy's treatment, shifting from active advocacy to emotional resignation as Andy embraces the absurdity of the situation.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: get Andy better treatment
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: emotionally overwhelmed, moving from physical action to passive acceptance
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: how to process his own frustration in the face of Andy's unexpected joy

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy's funeral film ends with a song, the casket vanishes behind a curtain, and mourners are left uncertain, some believing he might reappear, as silence falls.

Little Wendy comes over... and they comfort each other.

status_updates: present at pivotal moment in Andy's journey
auxiliary.persona_anchor: silent witness to Andy's final performance

focal_character_timelines.timeline_summary: Bob Zmuda evolves from Andy Kaufman's behind-the-scenes collaborator into a central figure in the orchestration and protection of his elaborate performance art. Initially a co-conspirator in hoaxes like Tony Clifton, Zmuda gradually assumes roles of protector, enabler, and emotional anchor as Andy's stunts grow more extreme and his health declines. The relationship shifts from creative partnership to caretaking, culminating in Zmuda's quiet stewardship of Andy's legacy after his death.

focal_character_timelines.selection_reason: Aligned with the movie's ICRP character set.

timeline_nodes.salient_development: George reveals that Andy and Tony Clifton are the same person, framing it as a business advantage to ABC executives.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: secure network approval for Andy's terms
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: positions himself as a credible advocate with influence over the network executives
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: whether the network will accept the dual identity claim and approve the deal
evidence_quotes: It's smart business! You'll get two Andy Kaufmans for the price of one!
George is confidentially whispering to them.
Maynard thinks about this, quite methodically. A beat, and then he cracks a smile...

relation_updates: positions himself as Andy's strategic advocate
status_updates: gains influence with ABC executives
auxiliary.persona_anchor: ability to transform confusion into leverage

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: George urges Andy to consider whether he's entertaining the audience or himself, emphasizing that experimental acts belong in cities like LA and New York, not regions with different expectations.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: George confronts Andy about audience walkouts and demands he consider whether his act serves the audience or his own artistic impulses.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: align Andy's performance style with regional audience expectations
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: Andy is left visibly uncertain about his motivation for performing
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: Andy does not know whether he is performing for the audience or for himself
evidence_quotes: What kind of show was this??! (angrily reading off a LIST) There were three-hundred walkouts! The promoter wants a refund!
When you play the Midwest and South, you DON'T MINDFUCK THESE PEOPLE! It's not postmodern -- it's rude.
If you wanna perform in Texas, you give 'em Mighty Mouse! You give 'em Elvis!!
Andy... you need to look inside: Who are you trying to entertain? The audience... or yourself?

relation_updates: asserts George's authority as a pragmatic counterbalance to Andy's artistic impulses
status_updates: Andy's experimental approach is formally questioned by management

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Tony Clifton is dragged offstage threatening revenge; Zmuda intercepts a reporter's camera but is exposed, passing it to George, who flees with the film.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: George receives the camera from Zmuda and flees the scene with the incriminating film, choosing to safeguard the footage rather than surrender it.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: to escape with the film and prevent public exposure
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: in possession of the film and actively fleeing the studio lot to avoid capture
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: what to do with the film and how to handle the consequences of being caught with it
evidence_quotes: Zmuda races by George, and suddenly palms off the camera into George's arms.
George grimaces, unclear about his loyalties. He sweatily peers at the timebomb in his hands.
George thrusts it under his jacket. He bolts for the door and feverishly scampers out.

relation_updates: reaffirms covert alliance with Zmuda
shifts loyalty back toward Kaufman's artistic subterfuge
status_updates: in possession of sensitive evidence
becomes a fugitive on the lot
auxiliary.persona_anchor: moral compromise in service of loyalty

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: George abruptly stops his convertible upon seeing a billboard announcing Andy Kaufman and Tony Clifton's one-night performance at Harrah's Tahoe, causing a chain reaction behind him.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: George abruptly stops his car after seeing a billboard advertising Andy Kaufman and Tony Clifton as co-performers, visibly stunned by the implication that the two identities are being presented as real and distinct.
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: George is left in a state of shock, confronted with a public representation that erases the boundary between Andy Kaufman and Tony Clifton he believed was clear.
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: the performance may further entrench the false separation between Kaufman and Clifton
evidence_quotes: something catches his eye -- and in shock he HITS the brakes
He's staring up at a BILLBOARD
It says "HARRAH'S TAHOE PRESENTS, ANDY KAUFMAN & TONY CLIFTON! TOGETHER ON STAGE! ONE NIGHT ONLY!"
George is flabbergasted

relation_updates: growing alienation from the public narrative
status_updates: publicly confronted with false representation
emotionally overwhelmed
auxiliary.persona_anchor: last defender of a collapsing truth

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy confirms he has rare lung cancer, distressing Lynne, who cries and asks about treatment and family; George remains skeptical, demanding truth, which Andy affirms.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: George presses Andy to confirm the truth of his cancer diagnosis, shifting from dismissal to acceptance after direct reassurance.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: verify whether Andy's cancer claim is true
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: George accepts Andy's diagnosis as real after demanding and receiving direct confirmation
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: how George will respond emotionally and practically to Andy's illness going forward
evidence_quotes: GEORGE That's ridiculous. You don't even smoke.
George and Zmuda glance skeptically at each other.
Confused, George leans in to Andy. GEORGE Andy... you look me in the eye, and tell me this is true.
ANDY George -- it's true.

relation_updates: deepens bond through moment of vulnerability and trust
status_updates: becomes a guardian of authenticity amid uncertainty
auxiliary.persona_anchor: voice of skepticism turned believer in the face of personal truth

timeline_nodes.scene_title: 148、INT. LAUREL CANYON HOUSE, LIVING ROOM - LATER THAT DAY
timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy seeks support to overcome negative energy and return to work with a triumphant show at Carnegie Hall, while George remains skeptical but cautiously supportive.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: George confronts Andy about the healer's lack of credibility and calls him the 'king of negative energy,' but ultimately asks how he can help after Andy breaks down.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: George resists endorsing Andy's delusions but acknowledges his emotional distress
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: George moves from silent disapproval to expressing skepticism directly, then shifts toward cautious engagement when Andy expresses fear and vulnerability
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: Whether George will continue to challenge Andy's beliefs or go along with his plans despite doubts
evidence_quotes: GEORGE What was that all about?
He glares, stewing.
GEORGE Andy, you're surrounded by what you create. You are the KING of negative energy.
So how can I help you...?

relation_updates: Tension escalates between George and Andy
George pushes back on Andy's self-image
status_updates: George recognized as a truth-teller in Andy's circle
auxiliary.persona_anchor: principled supporter in the face of delusion

timeline_nodes.scene_summary: Andy's funeral film ends with a song, the casket vanishes behind a curtain, and mourners are left uncertain, some believing he might reappear, as silence falls.
timeline_nodes.salient_development: George attends Andy's funeral, observes the mourners' confusion after the casket vanishes, and comments on the ambiguity of the moment as a fitting reflection of Andy's work.
timeline_nodes.goal_state: process Andy's death and make sense of the confusion
timeline_nodes.resulting_state: acknowledges Andy's absence while recognizing the lingering uncertainty surrounding his death
timeline_nodes.unresolved_issue: whether Andy's death is real or part of an ongoing performance
evidence_quotes: George gives Zmuda a hug.
George and Zmuda whisper.
GEORGE It's a perfect Kaufman audience.

relation_updates: shares grief with Zmuda
acknowledges Andy's enduring impact
status_updates: present at Andy's funeral
engaged in post-death communal uncertainty
auxiliary.persona_anchor: final witness to the ambiguity of Kaufman's life and art

focal_character_timelines.timeline_summary: George evolves from a skeptical talent observer to Andy Kaufman's loyal agent and emotional anchor, navigating layers of performance, deception, and personal crisis. He repeatedly confronts the blurring of reality and artifice, shifting from professional advocate to moral arbiter and caretaker. His journey culminates in grappling with Andy’s illness and death, where loyalty persists even amid uncertainty. The timeline traces his growing entanglement with Kaufman’s world, marked by moments of complicity, resistance, and ultimately, quiet acceptance.
